title: Multidimensional incentive compatibility and mechanism design
_reason: The paper addresses multidimensional incentive compatibility in mechanism design, a genuine economic question that in this literature (e.g., Crowell & Tran) is typically analyzed using tropical/min-plus convexity machinery.

title: A New Auction for Substitutes: Central-Bank Liquidity Auctions, 'Toxic Asset' Auctions, and Variable Product-Mix Auctions
_reason: This is Klemperer's product-mix auction work applying tropical/demand-types machinery to central-bank liquidity auction design, a genuine mechanism design economic application.

title: Extremal incentive compatible transfers
_reason: The paper studies incentive compatible transfers, a core mechanism design question, and 'extremal' transfers strongly suggest tropical/min-plus polyhedral machinery over the incentive-compatibility constraints characterizing implementable allocations.

title: MATHEMATICAL ENGINEERING TECHNICAL REPORTS Computing a Walrasian Equilibrium in Iterative Auctions with Multiple Differentiated Items
_reason: The paper addresses Walrasian equilibrium computation in auctions with indivisible differentiated items, a genuine economic problem, and such iterative auction/equilibrium work typically relies on discrete convexity/tropical machinery (M-convexity, min-plus structure).

title: Pricing in Resource Allocation Games Based on Duality Gaps
_reason: The paper addresses pricing in resource allocation games using duality gaps, which involves an economic question about pricing and strategic allocation, likely employing min-plus/tropical duality machinery, though without the abstract this is a tentative judgment based on the title's economic pricing content and optimization-duality framing.

title: Conception et tarification de nouveaux services en énergie dans un environnement compétitif
_reason: This thesis concerns designing and pricing new energy services in a competitive environment, aligning with the tropical/discrete-convexity pricing work of Akian, Bouhtou, Eytard & Gaubert on price incentives, addressing a genuine economic pricing/competition question.

title: A Geometric Approach to Mechanism Design
_reason: The paper applies geometric/tropical methods to mechanism design, a core economic problem involving incentives and mechanism/auction design.

title: Arctic Auctions, Linear Fisher Markets, and Rational Convex Programs
abstract: This paper unifies two foundational constructs from economics and algorithmic game theory, the Arctic Auction and the linear Fisher market, to address the efficient allocation of differentiated goods in complex markets. Our main contributions are showing that an equilibrium for the Arctic Auction is captured by a Rational Convex Program, and deriving the first combinatorial polynomial-time algorithm for computing Arctic Auction equilibria.
_reason: The paper studies auction equilibria and market allocation (genuine economic questions of pricing and competitive equilibrium) with 'Arctic' (tropical/min-plus-flavored) auction machinery linked to Fisher markets and rational convex programs.

title: Efficiently Restructuring Sovereign Debt via Arctic Auctions with Convex Costs
abstract: We study the problem of computing competitive equilibria in the Arctic product-mix auction, originally developed for the Icelandic government for exchanging blocked financial accounts, and more recently proposed by IMF staff for sovereign debt restructuring. From the buyers'perspective, the Arctic auction is equivalent to the quasi-linear Fisher market. However, unlike the standard Fisher model, the seller can express rich supply preferences through explicit supply-side costs and constraints. Despite extensive algorithmic literature on Fisher markets, the seller side has not received much attention, and no polynomial-time algorithm was previously known for computing competitive equilibrium when sellers face nontrivial costs. We examine the natural and expressive regime of separable, stepwise-increasing marginal costs that underlie the above-stated applications. Using polyhedral theory techniques, we first show that rational inputs lead to rational-valued competitive equilibria. Motivated by this result, we develop the first polynomial-time algorithm for this setting based on a non-trivial extension of classic primal-dual balanced-flow techniques for linear Fisher markets. Our work provides a robust computational foundation for auctions with sophisticated preferences, paving the way for flexible and institutionally feasible market designs in global finance.
_reason: The paper computes competitive equilibria in the product-mix (Arctic) auction with real economic applications (sovereign debt restructuring, Fisher markets, competitive equilibrium), building on the tropical-geometry-based product-mix auction framework.
